_meta.description: Ontology of statistical methods with assumptions, sample-size constraints, and when-to-refuse guidance for the Excel calc agent.

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
No extreme outliers distorting the average
whenToRefuse: All values are null or missing
methods.outputInterpretation: The arithmetic average of all values. Sensitive to outliers; consider median for skewed data.
caveats: Outliers can heavily distort the mean
Not meaningful for ordinal or nominal data

assumptions: Data is at least ordinal scale
whenToRefuse: All values are null or missing
methods.outputInterpretation: The middle value when data is sorted. Robust to outliers and skewed distributions.
caveats: Less efficient than the mean for normally distributed data
Not uniquely defined for even-sized samples without interpolation convention

whenToRefuse: All values are unique and continuous
All values are null or missing
methods.outputInterpretation: The most frequently occurring value. May be multimodal if several values share the highest frequency.
caveats: May not exist for continuous data with no repeats
Multiple modes possible
Not stable for small samples

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures the average spread of values around the mean. Uses Bessel correction (n-1) for sample SD.
caveats: Sensitive to outliers
Assumes symmetric spread for interpretability
Report whether sample or population SD is used

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
methods.outputInterpretation: The square of the standard deviation. Useful in analytical formulas but harder to interpret directly since units are squared.
caveats: Units are squared relative to the data
Sensitive to outliers

assumptions: Data is at least ordinal scale
methods.outputInterpretation: Difference between the maximum and minimum values. Simplest measure of spread.
caveats: Extremely sensitive to outliers
Increases with sample size
Not robust

assumptions: Data is at least ordinal scale
methods.outputInterpretation: The range between the 25th and 75th percentiles (Q3 - Q1). Robust measure of spread unaffected by outliers.
caveats: Ignores data outside the middle 50%
Different software may use different quartile interpolation methods

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
Sample is large enough for moment estimation
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures asymmetry of the distribution. Positive skew = right tail longer; negative skew = left tail longer. Near 0 suggests symmetry.
caveats: Highly sensitive to outliers
Small-sample estimates are unreliable
Report which formula (Fisher, Pearson) is used

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
Sample is large enough for fourth-moment estimation
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures tail heaviness relative to a normal distribution. Excess kurtosis > 0 = heavier tails (leptokurtic); < 0 = lighter tails (platykurtic).
caveats: Very sensitive to outliers
Small-sample estimates are unreliable
Report whether excess kurtosis or raw kurtosis is used

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
Summation is meaningful for the quantity
whenToRefuse: All values are null or missing
methods.outputInterpretation: The total of all numeric values in the range.
caveats: Missing values are excluded from the sum
Verify that summing the quantity makes semantic sense

methods.outputInterpretation: The number of non-null values in the range.
caveats: Null and empty cells are excluded by default
Clarify whether blanks or zeros should be counted

whenToRefuse: All values are null or missing
methods.outputInterpretation: The number of unique non-null values in the range.
caveats: Case sensitivity may affect results for text values
Floating-point precision may cause near-equal values to count as distinct

assumptions: Data is at least ordinal scale
methods.outputInterpretation: The value below which a given percentage of observations fall. The 50th percentile equals the median.
caveats: Different interpolation methods (linear, lower, higher, midpoint) yield slightly different results
Small samples make extreme percentiles unreliable

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
whenToRefuse: Trim percentage would remove all data
methods.outputInterpretation: Mean computed after removing a fixed percentage of extreme values from both tails. More robust than the arithmetic mean.
caveats: Choice of trim percentage is subjective
Typical trim is 5-25%

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
whenToRefuse: Winsorization percentage would replace all data
methods.outputInterpretation: Mean computed after replacing extreme values with the nearest retained value rather than removing them. Preserves sample size.
caveats: Choice of winsorization percentage is subjective
Typical winsorization is 5-25%

whenToRefuse: Any value is zero or negative
methods.outputInterpretation: The nth root of the product of n values. Appropriate for rates, ratios, and growth factors.
caveats: Undefined if any value is zero or negative
Always less than or equal to the arithmetic mean

whenToRefuse: Any value is zero or negative
methods.outputInterpretation: The reciprocal of the arithmetic mean of reciprocals. Appropriate for averaging rates (e.g., speeds, prices per unit).
caveats: Undefined if any value is zero
Strongly influenced by small values
Always the smallest of arithmetic, geometric, harmonic means

whenToRefuse: Mean is zero or near zero
methods.outputInterpretation: Ratio of standard deviation to mean, expressed as a percentage. Allows comparison of variability across datasets with different units or scales.
caveats: Meaningless when the mean is near zero
Not appropriate for interval-scale data (e.g., temperature in Celsius)

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
methods.outputInterpretation: Average absolute distance of each value from the mean. More robust to outliers than standard deviation.
caveats: Less commonly used than SD in inferential statistics
Clarify whether deviation is from mean or median

assumptions: Data is at least ordinal scale
methods.outputInterpretation: Median of the absolute deviations from the median. Very robust measure of spread; multiply by 1.4826 to estimate SD under normality.
caveats: Scaling constant depends on distributional assumption
Less efficient than SD for normal data

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
methods.outputInterpretation: Estimates the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the mean (SD / sqrt(n)). Decreases with larger samples.
caveats: Assumes independent observations
Not the same as standard deviation

assumptions: Data is interval or ratio scale
Approximately normal distribution or n >= 30 by CLT
whenToRefuse: Data is heavily skewed with small n
methods.outputInterpretation: An interval [lower, upper] that, at the chosen confidence level (e.g. 95%), is expected to contain the true population mean.
caveats: Does not mean there is a 95% probability the true mean is in this specific interval
Uses t-distribution for small samples
Assumes normality or relies on CLT for large n

assumptions: Data is continuous and approximately normal
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether the population mean differs from a specified value. Report the t-statistic, degrees of freedom, and p-value.
caveats: Robust to mild non-normality for n >= 30 via CLT
Two-tailed by default; specify if one-tailed is intended
Effect size (Cohen's d) should accompany the p-value

assumptions: Both groups are approximately normally distributed
Observations are independent within and between groups
Equal variances (homoscedasticity) for Student's t; use Welch's if unequal
whenToRefuse: Either group has fewer than 5 observations
Levene's test p < 0.01 and Welch correction not applied
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether two independent group means differ significantly. Report t-statistic, df, p-value, and confidence interval for the mean difference.
caveats: Use Welch's t-test when variances are unequal
Check normality in each group separately
Cohen's d should be reported for effect size

assumptions: Differences between pairs are approximately normally distributed
Pairs are independent of each other
No significant outliers in the differences
whenToRefuse: Differences are severely non-normal with small n
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether the mean difference between paired observations is significantly different from zero.
caveats: Analyze the differences, not the raw values
Robust to mild non-normality for n >= 30
Report effect size (Cohen's d for paired data)

assumptions: Both groups are approximately normally distributed
whenToRefuse: Either group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: A modification of the independent t-test that does not assume equal variances. Uses Satterthwaite approximation for degrees of freedom.
caveats: Preferred over Student's t-test when variance equality is uncertain
Slightly less powerful than Student's t when variances truly are equal
Generally recommended as the default two-sample t-test

assumptions: np >= 10 and n(1-p) >= 10 for normal approximation
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether the observed proportion differs from a hypothesized value. Report z-statistic and p-value.
caveats: Normal approximation; use exact binomial test for small samples
Continuity correction may improve accuracy for small n

assumptions: Observations are independent within and between groups
np >= 10 and n(1-p) >= 10 in both groups
whenToRefuse: Either group has np < 10 or n(1-p) < 10
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether two population proportions are equal. Report z-statistic, p-value, and confidence interval for the difference.
caveats: Use Fisher's exact test when sample sizes are small
Check that the normal approximation conditions are met

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether observed categorical frequencies match expected frequencies. Report chi-square statistic, df, and p-value.
caveats: Combine categories if expected counts are too low
Not appropriate for continuous data without binning
Large samples may yield significant results for trivial deviations

assumptions: Data is categorical in a contingency table
Expected frequency >= 5 in at least 80% of cells, no cell < 1
whenToRefuse: More than 20% of expected cell counts < 5
Table is 2x2 and any expected count < 5
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether two categorical variables are independent. Report chi-square statistic, df, p-value, and Cramer's V for effect size.
caveats: Use Fisher's exact test for 2x2 tables with small expected counts
Yates' continuity correction may be applied for 2x2 tables
Does not indicate direction of association

assumptions: Both distributions have the same shape (for testing medians)
whenToRefuse: Either group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Non-parametric test of whether one group tends to have larger values than the other. Report U statistic and p-value.
caveats: Tests stochastic dominance, not strictly medians, unless distributions have the same shape
Tied values require a correction
Less powerful than t-test when normality holds

assumptions: Differences are symmetric around the median
methods.outputInterpretation: Non-parametric alternative to the paired t-test. Tests whether the median of differences is zero.
caveats: Assumes symmetric distribution of differences
Zero differences are excluded by default
Less powerful than paired t-test under normality

assumptions: All groups have similar shape distributions
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Non-parametric alternative to one-way ANOVA. Tests whether at least one group distribution differs. Report H statistic, df, and p-value.
caveats: Does not indicate which groups differ; use post-hoc Dunn test
Tied values require a correction
Less powerful than ANOVA under normality

assumptions: Data is continuous and approximately normal in each group
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 5 observations
Levene's test p < 0.01 and Welch ANOVA not applied
Severe non-normality in any group with small n
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether at least one group mean differs from the others. Report F-statistic, df (between, within), p-value, and eta-squared.
caveats: Significant result does not indicate which groups differ; use post-hoc tests
Use Welch ANOVA when variances are unequal
Robust to mild non-normality with large, balanced groups

assumptions: Data is continuous and approximately normal in each cell
whenToRefuse: Any cell has fewer than 5 observations
Fewer than 2 levels in either factor
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests main effects of two factors and their interaction. Report F-statistics, df, p-values for each effect, and partial eta-squared.
caveats: Interaction effects should be interpreted before main effects
Unbalanced designs require Type III sums of squares
Post-hoc tests needed to identify specific group differences

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether data comes from a normal distribution. Report W statistic and p-value. p < 0.05 rejects normality.
caveats: Very powerful for small samples but overly sensitive for large n
Rejection does not mean the data is far from normal
Always supplement with Q-Q plots

assumptions: Comparison distribution is fully specified (parameters not estimated from data)
whenToRefuse: Parameters were estimated from the same data (use Lilliefors instead)
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether a sample comes from a specified distribution (one-sample) or whether two samples come from the same distribution (two-sample). Report D statistic and p-value.
caveats: Less powerful than Shapiro-Wilk for normality testing
Invalid if distribution parameters are estimated from data
Most sensitive to differences near the center of the distribution

whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether variances are equal across groups (homoscedasticity). Report F statistic, df, and p-value. p < 0.05 suggests unequal variances.
caveats: More robust than Bartlett's test for non-normal data
Use median-based variant (Brown-Forsythe) for skewed data
Large samples may yield significant results for trivially different variances

assumptions: Data is continuous and normally distributed in each group
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests equality of variances across groups assuming normality. Report chi-square statistic, df, and p-value.
caveats: Very sensitive to non-normality; prefer Levene's for non-normal data
Only use when normality has been verified

assumptions: Data is in a 2x2 contingency table
whenToRefuse: Table is not 2x2 (or use Freeman-Halton extension)
methods.outputInterpretation: Exact test of independence for 2x2 tables. Reports exact p-value and odds ratio.
caveats: Computationally intensive for large tables
Preferred over chi-square when expected counts < 5
Can be extended to RxC tables but is slow

methods.outputInterpretation: Exact test of whether observed proportion equals a hypothesized value. Report exact p-value and confidence interval.
caveats: Exact; no distributional approximation needed
Preferred over z-test for small samples
May be conservative for two-sided tests

assumptions: Repeated measures or matched groups design
methods.outputInterpretation: Non-parametric alternative to repeated-measures ANOVA. Tests whether at least one condition differs. Report chi-square statistic, df, and p-value.
caveats: Does not identify which conditions differ; use post-hoc Nemenyi or Conover test
Less powerful than repeated-measures ANOVA under normality

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests fit to a specified distribution with emphasis on tail behavior. Report A-squared statistic and p-value.
caveats: More sensitive to tail deviations than K-S test
Critical values depend on the hypothesized distribution
Available for normal, exponential, logistic, and other distributions

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests normality by combining skewness and kurtosis into an omnibus test. Report K-squared statistic and p-value.
caveats: Requires larger sample size than Shapiro-Wilk
Based on moments, so sensitive to outliers
Good general-purpose normality test for moderate to large samples

assumptions: Distribution parameters are estimated from the data
methods.outputInterpretation: Modified K-S test that accounts for parameter estimation from the sample. Report D statistic and p-value.
caveats: Use this instead of K-S test when parameters (mean, SD) are estimated from data
Less powerful than Shapiro-Wilk for small samples

assumptions: Data is paired binary observations in a 2x2 table
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests marginal homogeneity in paired 2x2 tables (e.g., before/after treatment). Report chi-square statistic and p-value.
caveats: Only uses discordant pairs (cells b and c)
Use exact binomial version when discordant count is small (<25)
Continuity correction may be applied

assumptions: Data is binary (0/1) across 3+ matched conditions
methods.outputInterpretation: Extension of McNemar's test to 3+ conditions. Tests whether proportions differ across matched conditions. Report Q statistic, df, and p-value.
caveats: Post-hoc pairwise McNemar tests needed to identify differing conditions
Requires binary response variable

whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether the medians of two or more groups are equal by comparing counts above and below the grand median. Report chi-square statistic, df, and p-value.
caveats: Less powerful than Kruskal-Wallis
Simple but wasteful of information since it only uses above/below counts
Useful when distributions differ in shape

assumptions: Preceded by a significant Kruskal-Wallis test
methods.outputInterpretation: Post-hoc pairwise comparison following Kruskal-Wallis. Report z-statistics and adjusted p-values for each pair.
caveats: Apply Bonferroni, Holm, or BH correction for multiple comparisons
Only meaningful after a significant omnibus test

assumptions: Preceded by a significant one-way ANOVA
methods.outputInterpretation: Post-hoc pairwise comparison controlling family-wise error rate. Report mean difference, confidence interval, and adjusted p-value for each pair.
caveats: Assumes equal sample sizes for optimal performance (use Tukey-Kramer for unequal sizes)
Controls Type I error at the specified alpha across all comparisons

assumptions: Multiple hypothesis tests are being performed
whenToRefuse: Only a single test is being performed
Number of comparisons is extremely large (consider BH/FDR instead)
methods.outputInterpretation: Adjusts the significance threshold by dividing alpha by the number of comparisons. Simple but conservative.
caveats: Very conservative with many comparisons; Holm or BH may be preferred
Applicable to any family of tests, not just ANOVA post-hoc

assumptions: Does not require equal variances or shapes
whenToRefuse: Either group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether P(X < Y) = 0.5, i.e. stochastic equality. More general than Mann-Whitney as it handles unequal variances and shapes.
caveats: Relatively modern test; not available in all software
Uses t-approximation for p-values
Preferred when distributional shapes differ between groups

assumptions: Linear relationship between X and Y
methods.outputInterpretation: Models Y as a linear function of X. Report coefficients, R-squared, residual standard error, and p-values.
caveats: Correlation does not imply causation
Extrapolation beyond the data range is unreliable
Check residual plots for assumption violations

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
methods.outputInterpretation: Models Y as a linear function of multiple predictors. Report adjusted R-squared, F-test, individual coefficient p-values, and VIF values.
caveats: Check for multicollinearity via VIF
Adjusted R-squared is preferred over R-squared for model comparison
Overfitting risk increases with many predictors relative to n

assumptions: Polynomial relationship between X and Y
whenToRefuse: No evidence of curvature in residuals
methods.outputInterpretation: Models Y as a polynomial function of X (degree 2+). Report coefficients, R-squared, and significance of higher-order terms.
caveats: High-degree polynomials overfit easily
Extrapolation is especially dangerous
Centered predictors reduce multicollinearity

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and log-odds
Large sample size relative to predictors
whenToRefuse: Fewer than 10 events per predictor (EPP rule)
methods.outputInterpretation: Models probability of class membership. Report odds ratios, confidence intervals, p-values, and AUC/ROC.
caveats: At least 10 events per predictor recommended
Separation issues cause infinite coefficients
Report AUC and calibration, not just p-values

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
methods.outputInterpretation: L2-regularized regression that shrinks coefficients toward zero without eliminating them. Report tuned lambda and cross-validated R-squared.
caveats: Does not perform variable selection; all predictors remain in the model
Lambda should be chosen via cross-validation
Coefficients are biased but have lower variance

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
methods.outputInterpretation: L1-regularized regression that shrinks some coefficients exactly to zero, performing variable selection. Report non-zero coefficients and tuned lambda.
caveats: May arbitrarily select one of correlated predictors
Lambda should be chosen via cross-validation
Unstable when predictors are highly correlated

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
whenToRefuse: Alpha and lambda not tuned via cross-validation
methods.outputInterpretation: Combines L1 and L2 penalties (LASSO + Ridge). Report mixing parameter alpha, tuned lambda, and selected features.
caveats: Two hyperparameters (alpha, lambda) require tuning
Handles correlated predictors better than pure LASSO

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
methods.outputInterpretation: Iteratively adds or removes predictors based on a criterion (AIC, BIC, p-value). Report final model and selection criterion values.
caveats: Inflates Type I error due to multiple testing
Results depend on starting model and direction
Prefer regularized methods (LASSO, elastic net) in modern practice

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
Outliers or heavy-tailed errors may be present
methods.outputInterpretation: Regression that down-weights large residuals using Huber's loss function. Report coefficients and standard errors.
caveats: Tuning parameter (delta) controls the threshold between L1 and L2 loss
Inference is approximate; bootstrap CIs may be preferred

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
A substantial fraction of data points are inliers
methods.outputInterpretation: Iteratively fits to random subsets and identifies inliers. Very robust to large outliers. Report inlier set and fitted model.
caveats: Stochastic; results may vary across runs
Performance degrades when outlier fraction is high
Not suitable for inference; use for exploratory/robust fitting

methods.outputInterpretation: Models conditional quantiles (e.g., median, 90th percentile) of Y given X. Report coefficients and confidence intervals at the specified quantile.
caveats: Does not assume a specific error distribution
Standard errors typically via bootstrap
Useful when interest is in tails or when heteroscedasticity is present

assumptions: Response is a non-negative integer (count data)
methods.outputInterpretation: Models count data as a function of predictors via log link. Report incidence rate ratios, confidence intervals, and goodness of fit.
caveats: Check for overdispersion; if present, use negative binomial
Zero-inflated models may be needed if excess zeros exist
Exposure/offset term may be required for rate data

assumptions: Response is a non-negative integer (count data)
methods.outputInterpretation: Models overdispersed count data with an additional dispersion parameter. Report incidence rate ratios, dispersion estimate, and goodness of fit.
caveats: Extra parameter compared to Poisson; slightly less efficient when Poisson is adequate
Zero-inflated variant may be needed for excess zeros

assumptions: Proportional hazards assumption (constant hazard ratio over time)
methods.outputInterpretation: Models the hazard of an event as a function of predictors. Report hazard ratios, confidence intervals, p-values, and concordance index.
caveats: Check proportional hazards assumption via Schoenfeld residuals
Does not estimate baseline hazard
At least 10 events per predictor recommended

whenToRefuse: Response has fewer than 3 ordered levels
methods.outputInterpretation: Models cumulative probabilities of ordinal categories. Report odds ratios for each predictor and test of proportional odds assumption.
caveats: Check proportional odds assumption via Brant test
Interpretation is for cumulative odds, not individual categories

methods.outputInterpretation: Models binary outcomes using the normal CDF link function. Report coefficients, marginal effects, and pseudo-R-squared.
caveats: Very similar to logistic regression; coefficients are not directly comparable
Marginal effects are more interpretable than raw coefficients
Less commonly used than logistic in most fields

assumptions: Response is continuous but censored (bounded at a threshold)
methods.outputInterpretation: Models a continuous response that is censored at a lower or upper bound (e.g., income data truncated at 0). Report coefficients and marginal effects.
caveats: Assumes normality of the latent variable
Misspecification of the censoring mechanism leads to biased estimates

whenToRefuse: No clustering structure in the data
methods.outputInterpretation: Models data with both fixed effects and random effects to account for hierarchical structure. Report fixed effects, random effect variances, and ICC.
caveats: Requires sufficient clusters (>= 5, preferably >= 20) for reliable random effect estimates
Model selection between random intercept and random slope can be complex
Report intraclass correlation (ICC)

assumptions: Non-constant variance or correlated errors with known structure
Observations may have heteroscedastic or correlated errors
whenToRefuse: Error structure is unknown and cannot be estimated
methods.outputInterpretation: Extension of OLS that models a non-scalar covariance structure of errors. Report coefficients, standard errors, and model fit.
caveats: Requires specification of the error covariance structure
Misspecified covariance can worsen estimates compared to OLS

assumptions: Heteroscedastic errors with known or estimable variance function
whenToRefuse: Weights are unknown and cannot be estimated
methods.outputInterpretation: Regression that weights observations inversely proportional to their variance. Report weighted coefficients and standard errors.
caveats: Requires correct specification of weights
Incorrect weights can produce worse estimates than OLS

assumptions: Window size is appropriate for the data frequency
whenToRefuse: Window size exceeds half the series length
methods.outputInterpretation: Smooths the series by averaging over a sliding window of k observations. Useful for identifying trends.
caveats: Introduces lag equal to half the window size
Choice of window size is subjective
Does not handle seasonality

methods.outputInterpretation: Smooths the series with exponentially decaying weights. More recent observations have higher influence. Report smoothing parameter (alpha).
caveats: Alpha must be in (0, 1); closer to 1 means less smoothing
Responds faster to changes than simple moving average
No explicit trend or seasonal components

methods.outputInterpretation: Measures correlation between the series and its lagged values. Useful for identifying MA order and seasonality.
caveats: Non-stationary data will show slowly decaying ACF
Confidence bands assume white noise
Differencing may be needed before interpretation

methods.outputInterpretation: Measures correlation at lag k after removing the effect of shorter lags. Useful for identifying AR order.
caveats: Interpret alongside ACF for ARIMA model identification
Sharp cutoff suggests an AR(p) process

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests for a unit root (non-stationarity). Null hypothesis: series has a unit root. Rejection suggests stationarity. Report ADF statistic and p-value.
caveats: Low power against near-unit-root alternatives
Lag selection affects results; use AIC/BIC
Consider structural breaks which can mimic unit roots

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests for stationarity. Null hypothesis: series is stationary. Rejection suggests a unit root. Report KPSS statistic and p-value.
caveats: Complementary to ADF (reversed hypotheses)
Use both ADF and KPSS together for robust conclusions
Bandwidth selection affects results

assumptions: Data has a regular time frequency
Sufficient observations to capture at least 2 full seasonal cycles
methods.outputInterpretation: Decomposes a series into trend, seasonal, and residual components. Report each component and specify additive vs. multiplicative model.
caveats: Additive when seasonal variation is constant; multiplicative when it scales with level
STL decomposition is preferred for robustness
End effects can distort the trend component

assumptions: Data has trend and/or seasonal components
methods.outputInterpretation: Forecasting method with level, trend, and seasonal components. Report smoothing parameters (alpha, beta, gamma) and forecast accuracy (MAPE, RMSE).
caveats: Additive vs multiplicative seasonality must be chosen
Does not handle complex patterns like ARIMA
Good baseline forecasting method

assumptions: Data is (or can be made) stationary after differencing
No seasonal pattern or seasonality has been removed
methods.outputInterpretation: Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average model. Report order (p,d,q), coefficient estimates, AIC/BIC, and forecast accuracy.
caveats: Model identification (p,d,q) can be challenging
Check residual diagnostics (Ljung-Box test, ACF of residuals)
Auto-ARIMA (AIC-based search) is recommended

assumptions: Data has both non-seasonal and seasonal patterns
methods.outputInterpretation: Extends ARIMA with seasonal terms (P,D,Q,m). Report full order, coefficients, AIC/BIC, and forecast accuracy.
caveats: Requires sufficient data to estimate seasonal parameters
Model selection is complex with many parameters
Seasonal period m must be correctly specified

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether past values of X improve prediction of Y beyond Y's own past. Report F-statistic and p-value for each lag.
caveats: Granger causality is predictive, not true causality
Sensitive to lag selection
Both series must be stationary or differenced to stationarity

assumptions: Individual series are I(1) (integrated of order 1)
A stable long-run equilibrium relationship may exist
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether non-stationary series share a common stochastic trend. Engle-Granger for bivariate; Johansen for multivariate. Report test statistic, critical values, and number of cointegrating relationships.
caveats: Series must be integrated of the same order
Johansen test is preferred for more than 2 series
Cointegration does not imply causation

assumptions: Applied to residuals from a fitted time series model
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether autocorrelations of residuals are jointly zero (i.e., residuals are white noise). Report Q statistic, df, and p-value. p < 0.05 suggests model inadequacy.
caveats: Choose lag carefully (common: min(10, n/5))
Degrees of freedom should account for estimated model parameters
Significant result means the model does not capture all autocorrelation

assumptions: Applied to residuals from a regression model
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests for first-order autocorrelation in regression residuals. Statistic ranges from 0 to 4; near 2 suggests no autocorrelation.
caveats: Only tests first-order (lag-1) autocorrelation
Inconclusive region exists between critical bounds
Invalid when lagged dependent variable is a predictor

assumptions: A potential structural change in parameters over time
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests for structural breaks in time series parameters (e.g., Chow test, Bai-Perron). Report break dates, test statistics, and p-values.
caveats: Multiple breakpoint algorithms exist; specify which is used
Pre-specifying the number of breaks vs. data-driven detection changes results
Breaks can be confused with unit roots

assumptions: Distribution is roughly symmetric for typical 1.5*IQR rule
methods.outputInterpretation: Flags observations below Q1 - 1.5*IQR or above Q3 + 1.5*IQR as outliers. Use 3*IQR for extreme outliers.
caveats: 1.5 multiplier is conventional but arbitrary
Identifies more outliers in non-normal distributions
Robust to outliers in the calculation itself

methods.outputInterpretation: Flags observations with |z| > threshold (typically 2.5 or 3) as outliers. Z = (x - mean) / SD.
caveats: Mean and SD are themselves affected by outliers (masking effect)
Assumes normality
For non-normal data use modified z-score or IQR

methods.outputInterpretation: Uses median and MAD instead of mean and SD: M = 0.6745 * (x - median) / MAD. Values with |M| > 3.5 are outliers.
caveats: Much more robust than standard z-score
MAD = 0 when more than 50% of values are identical
Threshold of 3.5 is conventional

assumptions: Tests one outlier at a time
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether the most extreme value is a significant outlier. Report G statistic, critical value, and p-value.
caveats: Only detects one outlier at a time
Multiple outliers can mask each other
Iterate after removing detected outliers (but use ESD for this)

methods.outputInterpretation: Tests the most extreme value in small samples. Report Q statistic and compare to critical value.
caveats: Designed for small samples (n = 3 to 30)
Tests only one outlier per application
Less powerful than Grubbs' for larger samples

assumptions: Upper bound on number of outliers is specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Detects up to k outliers simultaneously, avoiding masking. Report each test statistic, critical value, and the number of detected outliers.
caveats: Must specify maximum number of suspected outliers
Assumes approximate normality of the bulk of data
Preferred over repeated Grubbs' test

assumptions: Clusters have varying density but outliers are in low-density regions
methods.outputInterpretation: Density-based clustering that labels low-density points as noise/outliers. Report cluster assignments and noise point indices.
caveats: Requires epsilon (neighborhood radius) and minPts parameters
Performance depends on parameter tuning
Not designed for high-dimensional data

assumptions: Outliers are few and different from the majority
methods.outputInterpretation: Tree-based anomaly detection. Outliers are isolated in fewer splits. Report anomaly scores and a chosen contamination threshold.
caveats: Contamination parameter must be set (default ~0.1)
Stochastic; results may vary across runs
Works well for high-dimensional data

assumptions: Local density varies across the dataset
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures local density deviation. LOF >> 1 indicates outlier. Report LOF scores for each observation.
caveats: Requires k (number of neighbors) parameter
Computational cost grows with n
Captures local outliers that global methods miss

assumptions: Data is multivariate and approximately follows a multivariate normal distribution
whenToRefuse: Covariance matrix is singular or nearly singular
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures the distance of a point from the centroid accounting for correlations. Large distances indicate multivariate outliers.
caveats: Sensitive to non-normality
Covariance estimate is affected by outliers; consider robust covariance (MCD)
Threshold often based on chi-squared distribution

assumptions: Applied to a fitted regression model
whenToRefuse: No regression model has been fitted
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures the influence of each observation on regression coefficients. Values > 4/n or > 1 are commonly flagged as influential.
caveats: High Cook's D means the point influences the fit, not necessarily that it is an error
Thresholds (4/n, 0.5, 1) vary by convention
Examine flagged points rather than automatically removing them

assumptions: Local median is a reasonable baseline
methods.outputInterpretation: Identifies outliers using a rolling window median and MAD. Points deviating more than t*MAD from the local median are flagged.
caveats: Window size and threshold must be specified
Designed for time series but applicable to ordered data
Combines detection with optional replacement (by local median)

assumptions: Cash flows occur at regular intervals
Discount rate is known and constant
Cash flows are deterministic or expected values
methods.outputInterpretation: The sum of present values of all future cash flows minus the initial investment. NPV > 0 suggests the investment adds value.
caveats: Highly sensitive to the discount rate
Assumes cash flows can be reinvested at the discount rate
Does not account for project size differences

assumptions: Cash flows change sign at least once
Cash flows occur at regular intervals
whenToRefuse: No sign change in cash flows (no real IRR)
All cash flows are the same sign
methods.outputInterpretation: The discount rate that makes NPV = 0. Compare IRR to the required rate of return; IRR > hurdle rate suggests a worthwhile investment.
caveats: Multiple IRRs possible with non-conventional cash flows
Assumes reinvestment at the IRR (often unrealistic)
Use MIRR for non-conventional cash flow patterns

assumptions: Cash flows occur at regular intervals
Finance rate and reinvestment rate are specified
whenToRefuse: Finance and reinvestment rates not specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Resolves multiple-IRR problem by using separate rates for financing and reinvestment. Always yields a unique rate.
caveats: Requires explicit reinvestment and finance rate assumptions
More realistic than IRR but less commonly used

assumptions: Returns are measured at consistent intervals
whenToRefuse: Standard deviation of returns is zero
methods.outputInterpretation: Excess return per unit of total risk: (mean return - risk-free rate) / SD of returns. Higher is better.
caveats: Penalizes upside volatility equally with downside
Misleading for non-normal returns (heavy tails, skew)
Annualize by multiplying by sqrt(periods per year)

assumptions: Target return (usually risk-free rate) is specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Excess return per unit of downside risk: (mean return - target) / downside deviation. Focuses only on harmful volatility.
caveats: More appropriate than Sharpe when returns are asymmetric
Requires definition of target/minimum acceptable return
Downside deviation uses only negative deviations from target

assumptions: Beta is estimated from sufficient historical data
methods.outputInterpretation: Expected return = risk-free rate + beta * (market return - risk-free rate). Report expected return, beta, and alpha.
caveats: Single-factor model; multi-factor models (Fama-French) may be more accurate
Beta is unstable over time
CAPM is widely used but empirically imperfect

assumptions: Returns are measured at consistent intervals
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures sensitivity of asset returns to market returns. Beta = 1 means market-like risk; > 1 is more volatile; < 1 is less volatile.
caveats: Estimated via regression of asset returns on market returns
Unstable over time; use rolling windows
Assumes linear relationship with the market

methods.outputInterpretation: The maximum expected loss at a given confidence level over a specified time horizon. E.g., 95% VaR = 5th percentile of losses.
caveats: Does not describe the severity of tail losses beyond the threshold
Historical, parametric, and Monte Carlo methods give different results
Not subadditive; CVaR is preferred for risk aggregation

methods.displayName: Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR / Expected Shortfall)
methods.outputInterpretation: The expected loss given that the loss exceeds VaR. Captures tail risk severity. Always >= VaR.
caveats: More informative than VaR for tail risk
Subadditive (coherent risk measure)
Estimation requires sufficient tail observations

methods.outputInterpretation: The largest peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value. Expressed as a percentage. Indicates worst-case historical loss.
caveats: Backward-looking; does not predict future drawdowns
Longer observation periods tend to show larger drawdowns
Duration of drawdown (time to recovery) is also informative

assumptions: Both variables are continuous and interval/ratio scale
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two variables. r ranges from -1 to +1.
caveats: Only captures linear relationships; r = 0 does not mean independence
Very sensitive to outliers
Always visualize the relationship with a scatterplot

assumptions: Both variables are at least ordinal
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures the strength and direction of the monotonic relationship between ranks. rho ranges from -1 to +1.
caveats: More robust to outliers than Pearson
Captures non-linear monotonic relationships
Tied ranks require adjustment

assumptions: Both variables are at least ordinal
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures ordinal association based on concordant and discordant pairs. Tau ranges from -1 to +1.
caveats: More robust to ties and small samples than Spearman
Values tend to be smaller in magnitude than Spearman rho
Computationally more intensive for large n

assumptions: Continuous variable is approximately normal in each group
methods.outputInterpretation: Special case of Pearson r for one binary and one continuous variable. Mathematically equivalent to Pearson r in this case.
caveats: Effect of unequal group sizes on the maximum attainable correlation
Equivalent to an independent t-test for significance

methods.outputInterpretation: Measures association between two categorical variables. Ranges from 0 (no association) to 1 (perfect association).
caveats: Based on chi-square statistic
Bias-corrected version preferred for small tables
Does not indicate direction of association

methods.outputInterpretation: Measures association between two binary variables. Ranges from -1 to +1. Equivalent to Pearson r for two dichotomous variables.
caveats: Only for 2x2 tables; use Cramer's V for larger tables
Maximum absolute value depends on marginal distributions

methods.outputInterpretation: Measures the linear relationship between two variables after removing the effect of one or more control variables.
caveats: Assumes linearity of all relationships
Sensitive to outliers like Pearson r
Order of partialing can matter in complex models

assumptions: Both variables are continuous or high-dimensional
methods.outputInterpretation: Measures both linear and non-linear dependence. Ranges from 0 to 1. dCor = 0 if and only if the variables are independent.
caveats: Computationally intensive (O(n^2))
Always non-negative; does not indicate direction
More powerful than Pearson for detecting non-linear dependencies

assumptions: Adequate sample size relative to variables
methods.outputInterpretation: Reduces dimensionality by finding orthogonal components that maximize variance. Report eigenvalues, explained variance ratio, and loadings.
caveats: Standardize variables if they are on different scales
PCA is not a model; it is a transformation
Number of components to retain is subjective (scree plot, Kaiser criterion, cumulative variance)

assumptions: Clusters are spherical and roughly equal in size
Number of clusters k is specified
whenToRefuse: k is not specified and cannot be determined
methods.outputInterpretation: Partitions data into k clusters minimizing within-cluster sum of squares. Report cluster centers, sizes, and silhouette scores.
caveats: Results depend on initialization; use k-means++ or multiple restarts
k must be chosen (elbow method, silhouette, gap statistic)
Sensitive to outliers and non-spherical cluster shapes

assumptions: Sample is representative of the population
Observations are independent (or resampling scheme accounts for dependence)
whenToRefuse: Data has strong dependence structure not accounted for
methods.outputInterpretation: Resampling method to estimate the distribution of a statistic. Report bootstrap confidence interval (percentile, BCa, or basic) from B resamples.
caveats: Number of resamples B should be at least 1000 (10000 preferred)
BCa intervals are preferred over percentile intervals
Not a substitute for larger samples; garbage in, garbage out

assumptions: Under the null hypothesis, group labels are exchangeable
methods.outputInterpretation: Exact or approximate test by permuting group labels. Report the observed test statistic and permutation p-value.
caveats: Exact test is computationally expensive for large n; use random permutations
Number of permutations should be at least 1000
Very flexible; works with any test statistic

assumptions: A generative model or distributional assumptions are specified
whenToRefuse: No model or distribution is specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Simulates many scenarios from specified distributions to estimate probabilities, expectations, or confidence intervals. Report summary statistics across simulations.
caveats: Results depend on distributional assumptions
At least 10000 iterations recommended for stable estimates
Variance of the estimate decreases with sqrt(iterations)

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Sum of numeric values within each group. Useful for total revenue, counts, or cumulative measures per category.
caveats: Missing values are excluded
Groups with few observations may be misleading

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Arithmetic mean within each group. Report group sizes alongside means for context.
caveats: Susceptible to outliers within groups
Small group sizes yield unreliable means

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Median value within each group. More robust to outliers than the group mean.
caveats: Less efficient than mean for normal data
Small group sizes yield unstable medians

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Number of non-null observations in each group.
caveats: Null values are excluded
Verify whether blanks should count

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Number of unique non-null values in each group.
caveats: Case sensitivity may matter for text
Floating-point precision may cause near-equal values to count as distinct

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 2 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Standard deviation within each group. Report alongside means and group sizes.
caveats: Uses Bessel correction (n-1) by default
Sensitive to outliers within groups

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 2 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Variance within each group. Units are squared.
caveats: Squared units make direct interpretation harder
Sensitive to outliers

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Minimum value in each group.
caveats: May reflect outliers rather than typical values

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Maximum value in each group.
caveats: May reflect outliers rather than typical values

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 2 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Range (max - min) within each group.
caveats: Sensitive to outliers
Increases with group size

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 4 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Interquartile range within each group. Robust measure of within-group spread.
caveats: Requires sufficient observations per group for stable quartile estimates

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Percentile value not specified or out of range
methods.outputInterpretation: The specified percentile computed within each group.
caveats: Extreme percentiles are unreliable with small group sizes
Interpolation method may vary

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 8 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Skewness of the distribution within each group.
caveats: Unreliable for small groups
Sensitive to outliers

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 20 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Kurtosis of the distribution within each group.
caveats: Very unreliable for small groups
Sensitive to outliers

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
Group means are not near zero
whenToRefuse: Any group mean is zero or near zero
methods.outputInterpretation: CV (SD/mean) within each group. Allows comparison of variability across groups with different scales.
caveats: Meaningless when group mean is near zero
Requires ratio-scale data

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: Most frequent value within each group.
caveats: May not be unique
Not meaningful for continuous data with unique values

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: The first value encountered in each group based on row order.
caveats: Result depends on the sort order of the data
Not a statistical aggregate

assumptions: Data has a categorical grouping column
methods.outputInterpretation: The last value encountered in each group based on row order.
caveats: Result depends on the sort order of the data
Not a statistical aggregate

assumptions: Constraints are linear equalities or inequalities
Decision variables are continuous (or integer for ILP)
methods.outputInterpretation: Finds the values of decision variables that maximize or minimize a linear objective subject to linear constraints. Report optimal value and variable assignments.
caveats: Feasibility must be checked first
Integer constraints make the problem NP-hard (ILP)
Sensitivity analysis reveals how robust the solution is

assumptions: Function is continuous in the search region
methods.outputInterpretation: Finds the input value that makes the function equal to the target. Report the solution and residual error.
caveats: May converge to a local solution if multiple roots exist
Convergence depends on initial guess and method (bisection, Newton)
Function must be defined and continuous near the solution

assumptions: Objective and/or constraints may be nonlinear
Decision variables and bounds are specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Finds optimal values for a nonlinear objective subject to constraints. Report optimal value, variable assignments, and convergence status.
caveats: May find local optima, not global
Results depend on starting point
Multiple restarts recommended for non-convex problems

assumptions: A model with parameters is specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Examines how output changes when input parameters vary. Report parameter impacts (tornado chart, spider plot, or one-at-a-time tables).
caveats: One-at-a-time analysis misses interaction effects
Global sensitivity analysis (Sobol indices) is more thorough but costly
Results depend on the assumed parameter ranges

assumptions: A model with defined scenarios is specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Evaluates model output under predefined scenarios (e.g., best case, worst case, base case). Report results for each scenario.
caveats: Only as good as the scenarios defined
Does not cover the full parameter space
Complement with sensitivity or Monte Carlo for comprehensive analysis

assumptions: Data consists of paired observations or a single sample
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether the median of differences (or a single sample median) equals a hypothesized value by counting signs. Report number of positive and negative differences and p-value.
caveats: Less powerful than Wilcoxon signed-rank (ignores magnitude)
Zero differences are excluded
Very simple; makes minimal assumptions

assumptions: Data is a sequence of binary or dichotomized values
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether a sequence of two types is random. Too few or too many runs indicates non-randomness. Report number of runs, expected runs, and p-value.
caveats: Sensitive to the choice of dichotomization point
Less powerful than specific tests for serial correlation
Useful as a general randomness check

whenToRefuse: Either sample has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Tests whether two samples come from the same continuous distribution. Report D statistic and p-value.
caveats: Most sensitive to differences near the center of the distribution
Less powerful than Mann-Whitney for location shifts
Does not specify how distributions differ

assumptions: Prior on effect size is specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Computes a Bayes factor quantifying evidence for H1 vs H0. BF10 > 3 is moderate evidence for H1; BF10 > 10 is strong. Also reports posterior distribution of the effect size.
caveats: Results depend on the chosen prior
Bayes factor is sensitive to prior width (Jeffreys-Zellner-Siow prior is common)
Does not require correction for multiple comparisons like frequentist tests

assumptions: Prior on proportion is specified (Beta prior)
methods.outputInterpretation: Computes the posterior distribution of the proportion using a Beta-Binomial conjugate model. Report posterior mean, credible interval, and Bayes factor if comparing to a specific value.
caveats: Posterior depends on the chosen prior (Beta(1,1) = uniform is common)
Credible intervals have a direct probability interpretation unlike frequentist CIs

methods.outputInterpretation: Computes the posterior distribution of the correlation coefficient. Report posterior median, 95% credible interval, and Bayes factor for H1: rho != 0 vs H0: rho = 0.
caveats: Prior choice affects results, especially for small samples
Uniform prior on rho in [-1,1] or stretched beta prior are common choices

assumptions: Linear relationship between predictors and response
Priors on coefficients and error variance specified
methods.outputInterpretation: Provides posterior distributions for all regression coefficients and error variance. Report posterior means, credible intervals, and model comparison via Bayes factors or WAIC.
caveats: Requires specification of priors (weakly informative priors are common)
Computational cost may be higher than frequentist approaches
Model comparison via Bayes factors or information criteria (WAIC, LOO-CV)

whenToRefuse: Any group has fewer than 5 observations
methods.outputInterpretation: Computes Bayes factors comparing models with and without group effects. Report BF for inclusion of each factor and posterior distributions of group means.
caveats: Prior on effect sizes affects BF; default Cauchy prior is common
Can compare multiple models simultaneously
No multiple comparison correction needed as in frequentist ANOVA
